text.fullText: Rincon Band of Luiseño Indians One Government Center Lane | Valley Center | CA 92082 (760) 749-1051 | Fax: (760) 749-8901 | rincon-nsn.gov VIA FEDERAL ERULEMAKING PORTAL ONLY Federal eRulemaking Portal: https://www.regulations.gov/ February 3, 2026 Administrator Lee Zeldin U.S. Environmental Protection Agency 1200 Pennsylvania Avenue, N.W. Washington, DC 20460 RE: COMMENTS ON PROPOSED RULE REVISING CLEAN WATER ACT SECTION 401 REGULATIONS DOCKET ID NO. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 Dear Administrator Zeldin: On behalf of the Rincon Band of Luiseño Indians (“Rincon” or “the Tribe”), we submit the following comments in response to the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency’s proposed rule revising the Clean Water Act (CWA) Section 401 water quality certification regulations. Rincon is a federally recognized Indian tribe with inherent sovereignty and a solemn responsibility to protect the water resources that sustain our people, lands, culture, and future generations. Clean water is inseparable from the Tribe’s public health, cultural practices, spiritual traditions, and long-standing uses of aquatic resources. Accordingly, Rincon exercises its authority under the CWA as a sovereign co-regulator and relies on the United States to uphold its trust responsibility and government-to-government obligations. While EPA characterizes this proposal as restoring Section 401 to its “proper statutory purpose,” Rincon is concerned that the proposed rule would instead weaken one of CWA’s core safeguards by constraining the lawful authority of states and authorized tribes to protect water quality. The Tribe is particularly troubled by the proposal’s emphasis on limiting the scope of review and conditions in the name of efficiency, at the expense of environmental protection and tribal self-determination. Section 401 was deliberately designed by Congress to provide states and tribes with a meaningful check on federally licensed or permitted activities that may degrade water quality. For tribes, this authority is essential given the cumulative impacts of development, historic pollution burdens, limited land bases, and the downstream nature of many tribal waters. The ability to Steve Stallings Joseph Linton Laurie E. Gonzalez John Constantino Alfonso Kolb, Sr. Chairman Vice Chairman Council Member Council Member Council Member Comments on Proposed Rule Revising Clean Water Act Section 401 Regulations February 3, 2026 Page 2 evaluate site-specific and project-specific impacts is not regulatory “overreach,” but rather the precise function Congress intended Section 401 to serve. Rincon strongly opposes regulatory revisions that would narrow the scope of water quality review, restrict the bases on which certification conditions may be imposed, or otherwise limit tribal discretion to protect designated uses, aquatic resources, culturally significant waters, or downstream tribal waters. As clarified during EPA’s public webinar on the proposed rule, the revisions would eliminate standalone Treatment as a State authority for Section 401, impose rigid procedural requirements that do not account for tribal capacity constraints, and emphasize uniformity over tribal judgment. Collectively, these changes would significantly impair tribes’ ability to meaningfully certify projects under Section 401 and would reduce the provision from a substantive safeguard to a largely procedural exercise for many tribes. Although the Rincon Band of Luiseño Indians has obtained Treatment as a State authority under Section 303(c) of the CWA, and has EPA-approved Tribal Water Quality Standards effective January 30, 2024, the proposed revisions would still constrain the Tribe’s ability to meaningfully exercise its Section 401 certification authority. By tying certification decisions and conditions narrowly to EPA-approved water quality standards, the proposal reduces the Tribe’s discretion to address site-specific, short-term, and cumulative impacts that may not be fully captured by existing standards. Even where Rincon seeks to impose reasonable, protective conditions grounded in its water quality standards and designated uses, the proposed framework increases the likelihood of federal second-guessing and applicant challenges, rather than deferring to the Tribe’s expertise regarding local conditions and culturally significant waters. The Tribe is also concerned that the proposed rule prioritizes project timelines and permitting certainty over meaningful tribal participation and informed decision-making. Many tribes, including Rincon, face capacity and resource limitations and depend on complete, accurate, and timely information from applicants and federal agencies. Compressed timelines and reduced procedural flexibility increase the risk that the Tribe may be forced to act on incomplete information or face procedural waiver of its certification authority, undermining meaningful tribal participation and the government-to-government relationship. EPA’s repeated framing of Section 401 as having been “weaponized” by states and tribes is deeply troubling. This characterization disregards the legitimate exercise of sovereign authority and the lived experience of tribal communities that bear the environmental consequences of federally approved projects. Tribes are not seeking to block development for reasons unrelated to water quality; they are seeking to fulfill their statutory and moral duty to protect water resources as required by the CWA. For these reasons, Rincon urges EPA to substantially revise or withdraw the proposed rule. At a minimum, EPA must: • Explicitly affirm the full authority of authorized tribes under Section 401 on equal footing with states; Comments on Proposed Rule Revising Clean Water Act Section 401 Regulations February 3, 2026 Page 3 • Preserve tribal discretion to address all water quality-related impacts consistent with the CWA and applicable tribal water quality standards; • Reject limitations that would prevent tribes from protecting culturally significant waters, downstream resources, or designated uses; and • Engage in robust, meaningful government-to-government consultation with tribes prior to finalizing any revisions to the Section 401 regulations. Absent these changes, the proposed rule risks undermining the CWA’s protective purpose, eroding tribal sovereignty, and weakening environmental protections for some of the nation’s most vulnerable waters. Thank you for the opportunity to submit these comments. Rincon expects EPA to fully consider tribal concerns and to honor its trust responsibility and consultation obligations as it proceeds with this rulemaking. Sincerely yours, RINCON BAND OF LUISEÑO INDIANS Steve Stallings Tribal Chairman Copy to: Camille Merchant, Rincon Band Environmental Director, cmerchant@rincon-nsn.gov Denise Turner Walsh, Rincon Band Attorney General, dwalsh@rincon-nsn.gov
